[194:54]You have to understand, this is a catastrophic defeat, which tangibly hurts the United States.

[195:01]We are effectively caught in a bear trap, Trump tried to kill Iran, he failed, and now what

[195:09]Iran is doing is they are trying to impose the maximum cost on not only the United States,

[195:16]but everybody else in the region and also in the entire world.

[195:28]Iran wants this to go on for a pretty long time, not forever and not indefinitely.

[195:33]But Iran wants this to hurt, and for it to hurt, it needs to go on for longer than we're

[195:39]comfortable with, and they need to make it hurt badly.

[195:44]Badly for the regional players that assisted the United States,

[195:48]badly for the United States, which has done the bulk of the fighting,

[195:52]and also badly for all the other regions of the world that will be affected by the price increases.

[195:58]And the reason they're doing that, naturally,

[196:01]is so that the United States, Israel, and every other country will avoid ever attacking Iran again.

[196:09]And so we are stuck by design. We were drawn into a trap by Iran and by Israel, interestingly

[196:17]enough, so that there is no escape hatch. There's no exit plan for the United States.

[196:23]And that's a feature.

[196:25]So, that is the state of the conflict.

[196:28]That doesn't mean that Trump is not seeking a way out.

[196:31]As I said, Trump and the military planners are trying to get creative.

[196:35]They're looking for a way that we can leverage Iran just enough that we can get out of this.

[196:42]how do you change this equation? Well, so far, our attacks and our weapons are not

[196:50]able to achieve the objectives that we set out to accomplish in the war. And so

[196:56]we have effectively had to admit defeat, that we are not going to topple Iran's

[197:00]regime, that we're not going to be able to destroy all of their missile launch

[197:04]platforms. We may not even be able to reopen the Strait of Hormuz.

[197:15]looking for a cheap and easy win, or maybe a risky win, maybe a gamble, but they're looking

[197:22]for a smaller win.

[197:23]They're looking for a win on the battlefield, and they're looking to increase their leverage

[197:28]ever so slightly, not so that we couldn't claim total victory, not so that we can run

[197:37]But so that we don't have to accept such a humiliating defeat so obviously on Iran's terms.

[197:44]So Trump is looking for something he can do to up the ante so that one we could say, well,

[197:50]we won this battle.

[197:52]Now we're ending the conflict on our terms.

[197:54]And that has a purely rhetorical significance.

[197:58]But also from a tactical point of view, trying to gain a little bit of leverage over Iran

[198:04]so that in reality, in a tangible way, we actually don't have to accept all of Iran's

[198:09]terms.

[198:15]The regime remains intact.

[198:22]they might even keep the Strait of Hormuz. But maybe we'll be able to get a

[198:27]little something, just a little something to show for it. And how is Trump

[198:32]seeking to do that?

[198:55]And this will force Iran's hand either by making them negotiate for control over their

[211:44]Well, I would say about the Strait of Hormuz.

[211:47]There's something you need to understand

[211:49]about the economic pain that is being applied by Iran.

[211:53]And that is that the sharing of economic pain is not even.

[211:59]It's not even chronologically, and it's not even even economically either.

[212:06]The pain that results from the closure of the Strait of Hormuz will be felt first by

[212:12]some countries and later by other countries, and why is that?

[212:17]It has to do with the final destination of the energy products.

[212:27]The oil from Iran, Iraq, Kuwait, the Emirates, Saudi Arabia, all of that oil for the most

[212:36]part is exported to India, China, South Korea, Taiwan, Japan, Malaysia, the Philippines.

[212:43]This is where the oil actually goes first.

[212:46]So if that oil is not going out from the straight, there's a couple of things that are happening.

[212:51]And the first thing that is happening is that these countries are not going to get their

[212:58]That is what happens first

[213:01]The actual oil in the barrels that's pumped out of Iran and Saudi Arabia

[213:06]It just stops showing up to India and that actually hasn't started happening yet because there's a timed delay

[213:14]The oil that left the straight before the war is still out at sea and is still arriving in ports

[213:21]They're still getting it. They're still getting their jet fuel and diesel and gap their petrol

[213:26]They're still getting all those products. They're still getting it and they're still able to have energy

[213:32]But it's on a timed delay and once all those shipments are completed. There's going to be a gap

[213:41]in the oil import from the Persian Gulf.

[213:45]This is why the prices are going up

[213:47]and that's a secondary effect.

[213:49]Because of course, what are these countries going to do?

[213:52]Well, they still need energy.

[213:54]They still need fossil fuels, so they're just going to buy it from somewhere else.

[214:00]They'll buy it from another supplier.

[214:02]And this is why the prices go up.

[214:05]Because the supply is constrained while demand remains the same.

[214:10]So if there is the same demand, but there's a smaller supply, then the price goes up.

[214:15]And that's why it affects us.

[214:18]But we still are getting oil.

[214:20]We still are getting oil from Canada,

[214:22]and we're still getting oil from other sources.

[214:25]And so we're only hit by the price shock.

[214:28]We're actually not affected necessarily

[214:30]in the same way by this supply crunch.

[226:30]And fundamentally, it's about overexpansion.

[226:34]The United States is still unquestionably a first-tier great power.

[226:44]But we are no longer a hyper power. We are no longer a global hegemon.

[226:54]We can't pressure China.

[226:56]And increasingly, we can't even pressure

[226:58]these middle powers effectively, North Korea, Iran.

[227:05]maximum pressure sanctions.

[227:09]a strategic weapon in the case of North Korea,

[227:11]or if they have a conventional arsenal

[227:14]of strategic weapons like Iran,

[227:16]there is little in the way of military action

[227:18]that we can even do.

[227:20]So, this is overestimating our capability.

[227:24]This is overextension.

[227:26]We're writing checks that we can't cash.

[227:28]It doesn't mean that we're not a powerful nation.

[227:31]It doesn't mean we don't have leverage.

[227:33]It doesn't mean that that leverage is not powerful.

[227:37]to persuade or leverage other countries to do what we want them to do.

[227:48]We can't retake Crimea and the Donbas.

[227:51]We can't prevent Iran from closing the Strait of Hormuz.

[227:55]We can't achieve regime change.

[227:57]We just simply can't.

[228:05]every time that happens, it erodes deterrence.

[228:14]as a paper tiger.

[228:16]They cannot really and truly follow through on its threats.
